source.title: Linux Basics for Hackers — Chapter 2: Text Manipulation
source._notes: Demo content: a small, faithfully-paraphrased excerpt of one chapter, for prototype validation only. The full book by OccupyTheWeb is published by No Starch Press and is copyrighted — production use of this material requires publisher licensing. See README.

flags: code blocks were detected in raw extraction and treated as code (not paraphrased)

story.title: Finding the needle: filtering streams with grep

blocks.text: When a terminal gives you too much

Linux command-line tools are generous: they hand you the whole file, the whole log, the whole configuration. Most of the time you only care about the few lines that match a keyword. That is the job of grep — described in the source as probably the most widely used text manipulation command in Linux.
grep lets you filter the content of a file for display. Give it a keyword and a source of text, and it returns only the lines that contain that keyword. Everything else is hidden.
grep reads a stream of text and prints only the lines that match the keyword you give it.
Pipes: how two commands cooperate

The classic way to use grep is to combine it with another command using a pipe — the vertical bar character |. The pipe takes whatever the command on the left prints and feeds it as input to the command on the right.

In the source's example, the goal is to see every line in the Snort configuration file that mentions the word output. cat reads the whole file; the pipe sends that output to grep, which keeps only the matching lines:
kali> cat /etc/snort/snort.conf | grep output
blocks.caption: Listing 2-5: Display only lines containing the keyword “output”

blocks.text: The result is a much shorter view: instead of hundreds of lines of configuration, you see just the lines that match. The same idiom — something | grep keyword — works on log files, command output, anything that produces text.

blocks.title: Why pipes are a superpower
blocks.text: Because every Unix command reads input and prints output, pipes let you chain commands together into custom workflows without writing a program. grep, sort, head, tail, wc — they all compose this way.
Combining grep, nl, head, and tail

Once you can chain commands, you can answer surprisingly specific questions. The source poses a small challenge: display the five lines immediately before the line that contains # Step #6: Configure output plugins.

The trick has two stages. First, use nl (number lines) piped into grep to find the line number of the marker — the source shows it lives at line 512. Then use tail to start reading from line 507 and pipe that into head to keep just the first six lines:
kali> tail -n+507 /etc/snort/snort.conf | head -n 6
blocks.caption: Return the marker line plus the five lines before it

blocks.text: Small composable tools beat one big tool: nl finds positions, tail seeks from a line, head limits the count, grep filters by content.

questions.prompt: What does the grep command do?
options: It edits a file in place by replacing matching text
It filters a stream of text and prints only the lines that match a keyword
It compresses files to save disk space
It runs a Linux service in the background
questions.explanation: grep takes text input and returns only the lines containing the keyword you specify; everything else is dropped from the display.

questions.prompt: What is the role of the pipe character | in the command cat file.conf | grep output?
options: It runs both commands at the same time on different files
It separates two unrelated commands so they each show their own output
It sends the output of the command on the left into the command on the right as input
It tells grep to ignore the file and print everything
questions.explanation: The pipe connects commands: cat prints the file's contents, and the pipe feeds those contents to grep as its input.

questions.prompt: In the chapter's challenge, why combine tail and head instead of using a single command?
options: Because grep cannot match line numbers
Because tail starts reading from a given line, and head limits how many lines to keep — together they slice a window out of the file
Because Linux requires every pipeline to contain at least two commands
Because head reverses the order of the lines
questions.explanation: tail -n+507 starts reading from line 507; piping into head -n 6 keeps just the first six of those lines, giving you the marker plus the five lines preceding it. Each tool does one thing; the pipeline does the composite job.

lessons.title: Using sed to Find and Replace

story.title: Search-and-replace from the command line: meet sed

blocks.text: When a GUI editor is the wrong tool

Opening a text editor to change one word in a configuration file is fine when you have one file. It is misery when you have fifty. The Linux command sed exists for exactly this: search for a word or text pattern, then perform an action on every match — all without opening the file in an editor.

The source describes sed as a tool that lets you look for occurrences of a word or pattern and then act on them. Its name is short for stream editor, which captures the idea: sed edits a stream of text as it flows past, line by line.
sed is non-interactive search-and-replace for files and streams — write the rule once, apply it everywhere.
Why this matters for everyday Linux work

Pair sed with grep and the pipe character you just learned and you can do remarkably powerful things: filter a long file down to the lines you care about, then rewrite those lines in place. This is the foundation that scripting and automation are built on later in the book.

blocks.title: A pattern you will see again
blocks.text: Every command in this chapter — grep, nl, head, tail, sed — does one job. The power comes from combining them with pipes. Memorize the pieces; combinations follow.

options: Opening files in a graphical editor
Searching for occurrences of a word or pattern and performing an action on them
Compressing log files
Encrypting text files at rest
questions.explanation: The source defines sed as a command that searches for a word or text pattern and then performs an action on it — most commonly, replacing it.

questions.prompt: Why does the chapter present commands like grep, head, tail, and sed together?
options: They are all variants of the same command
Each does one specific job, and they compose with pipes to handle larger tasks
Only one of them actually works on Kali Linux
They all require a graphical interface to run
questions.explanation: The unifying lesson of the chapter is that small single-purpose tools combine through pipes — that is the basis of Linux's command-line philosophy.
